Hey, it's Andre and Sean. Today we are talking about a 10,000 foot view of the product launch series. This is the second part of the art of email revenue engine along with the story powered promotions. I'm going to have, I'm going to take the similar role that I've done in the past, which is I will play the role of the audience, ask more questions and have Andre open things up with an explanation of what we see on screen here. So Andre, tell us what we're looking at. We'll start with the top. We'll go through that and then we can jump down to the bottom two thirds. Right. So the top just represents the framework of every PLS. So it's a sequence or series of emails, the play out over, over a week, two weeks. Yeah. So and we get people into the segment by them raising the hands, using a hard bridge. So in this image here, there's two, you can send a single one. In some cases and other cases, you would send two. This isn't a rule. This is just, you know, what, what feels right. And from these people are going to segment themselves out. So you'll, you'll have an idea, like a signal of how many people you're going to be, you know, exposing this PLS to. And then it'll run its course and it has a natural hard deadline at the end. And then obviously the revenue shows up at the point of wherever the cart open is. So this is, you know, broadly speaking, it's, it's the whole thing. And at some point, so let's just say on E4, if we, if you want to think about it this way, that's the cart open. This is the pre-launch, maybe these three, this is the cart open. And then this is the main, the main PLS, at which point revenue will be generated from the first email all the way through to the last with a sag in the middle. So Yeah, the trough of despair. And I think it's something you just said is interesting to draw attention to, because you can see in the, in the diagram, there's an A to B tension, you know, that structural tension between where the audience is and where you're taking them. And that, that there's that unifying theme or narrative arc of an entire PLS. But within a PLS, there are two significant inflection points. The first in the first is a relief of tension, a very significant release of tension, which is when a cart opens and somebody can purchase. So there's sort of this, you know, could be if, if on E4 is the day it opens, and we have three emails leading up to a natural release of tension, it has to be managed within a PLS. We have to think about that when we're creating them, because we have, we don't, we don't want to have all of the tension gone on E4 on that fourth day, cart opens, and it's kind of over, like, what do we do now? And then try to recapture the tension on the last day, when that's the other natural point of tension in the PLS. There's a beginning and there's a, there's a beginning of the series. There is the end of the series and the end of the promotion. And there's in somewhere in, in between those two things is a point at which somebody can buy. So the pre launch, the initial emails are building up, they're naturally creating tension because they can't take action. The person reading it can't do anything yet. When they can reply, they can send you feedback, they can answer questions, there's little releases of tension. But once cart opens, that is a very significant release of tension. And that has to be managed, because you don't want the entire promotion, you don't want to pack everything into the first four days, and then wonder, well, what do I, what do I do now? And that's, you have many thoughts on how to manage that, that release of tension and to maintain it. Because we don't do, there are ways to do that, you know, you'll see people, they add bonuses and they, all kinds of monkey business toward the end, we don't do any of that. So to maintain tension throughout, that's, that's the, that's the writing, the emails are doing that, the messaging and the theme and the narrative arc maintains that attention, that tension, and captures attention along the way. Any thoughts about that and how, how do, how we continue to pull attention forward after that, you know, once cart opens? How we can manage the, the squirt of dopamine, that's happening over here. Every recording has to have a reference to a squirt of dopamine, right? I mean, and when cart opens, that's the, that's the big squirt, right? That's, that's the first one where it's like, oh, I can do this thing now, there's a, I can go buy the thing. And as you said, that's, you know, we have day one and the opening day and the last day are when you'll, in general, if our experience is any indicator, those are when you have the majority of sales, somewhere between 30 and 40% on day one, or 30 and 40% on the last day, and then in between is the difference. So in between maybe the remaining 30% or whatever it is, 20%. And then there will, there will be periods, I think this is important to mention too, after the first day, if that's your first, first PLS, first day, like this is incredible. And if you, if you assume that every other day after that's going to look the same, you're likely to be highly disappointed of that first day or the first movers, right? And then the last day are the people who have been waiting to the end. In between, there are often days when we talk, we use the term before, there are some days when it's just despair. You just look at like, it's over, it's the whole thing has failed. It's just a natural part of the PLS. It's a natural part of human behavior. First movers, there's a portion of your audience who are first movers who want to get it immediately, and a portion who are waiting until the end, before the deadline closes, and then everything else happens in between sort of an asymmetric way. Anything else to mention too, you're on this top one before we move on to the more in depth explanation? Yeah, what you just said, you know, that the revenue spark on Cod Open is significant, and including the last day. So that's what this little image just represents. It's the, over here, right in the middle is the trough of despair. So that's what that's what that means. One thing that's worth mentioning over here, you know, we're just using E4 as a, to demonstrate the point that there's a pre-launch, and then there's a Cod Open, it doesn't have to be on E4. And we've got many examples. We've shared lots of our examples of real PLSs in this module. Some of them are short, and some of them are longer. If there's a PLS for, you know, the most obvious one is if you do a Black Friday. They're pretty short, because, you know, it's Friday. It's Black Friday is where the Cod opens, and we normally run ours through to Southern Monday, because, you know, it's part of the excuse. I've done them on a single day before. The whole thing open, closes on a single day, and in the past I've done that for my birthday as well. So there will be a few pre-launch emails, and then it's my birthday. So my entire PLS is just very, very compact. It's, and it's real, you know, it's Cod opens at, you know, one second after midnight, the day before, and then it closes. And so for the Black Fridays, if you want to run through to the Mondays, you know, then it really just runs over the weekend. And a few pre-launch emails. So those ones can be very, very small and compact, and then you can get much longer ones. I think some of the ones we've shared, like the Traffic Engine one, they, you know, they were big. They were fairly significant. And one thing to call attention to here that I didn't think about, I mean, just mentioned it, made me think about it, which is these, because they're labeled sequentially, does not mean that they have to be contiguous days. So you can see in the, in the bottom, for example, that we didn't send emails over a weekend. So this just, the top diagram just shows that this is the number of emails in a series, but it doesn't necessarily have to be one day right after another. So it's not necessarily a 10-day timeframe on the calendar. It could be, you know, for example, this one could be a Monday through Friday and then another Monday through Friday, which is what we did for the Art of Email and the Live Workshop enrollment. That was the format that we just didn't send over the weekend, or you could send over a weekend. And particularly, a lot of that comes down to the texture of the, of the, the enrollment itself, because if you have, for example, if Monday through Wednesday is pre-enrollment part of it, and then you open card on a Thursday and you close it on a Monday, you may very well want to send emails over the weekend, or not. It depends entirely on that, through how you're thinking about it. But, and if you're, if you're going like we did with the Art of Email Workshop, starting on a Monday and closing card the following Friday, well, we didn't, we didn't feel like we needed to use the weekend for that. So if there's no right answer, I think it's part of this. It's like everything we talk about. There's no, there's no definitive how many do you need. Just be aware that maintaining the attention of an audience over 10 days, over two weeks, you know, 10 emails, that is, that's a lot. That's a lot for everybody. It's a lot for the audience, and it's a lot for you as the creator. So just be aware of what you're getting yourself into. And I do think there may be, you know, maybe at the, at the lower end, like a single day promotion has, it has its own characteristics, it has its own textures and flavor around that. That one is probably the, I, from my perspective, I think that's probably the hardest one to pull off because we just, the, to get, there's more work needs and we try to get people to pay attention at exactly the right time when that window opens, closes so fast. So each, each decision we make about how long the actual promotion is reverberates throughout the requirements of the PLS, because if it's, if carts open for two weeks, you're not going to send, I don't think you're going to send emails every day for two weeks. That's, that's a lot for everybody. If it's open for 24 hours, you probably need to do a little more work in the beginning to make sure that everybody who's interested knows what's coming and knows that it's open and closed in 24 hours. And so each one has its own requirements, I think. Anything to add on that top one before we move on to the example at the bottom, which is the, that's the, this is the, sort of the diagram of the, the two PLS as we ran simultaneously for the R2VMail live workshop. I'm going to move on to that, or do you want to, anything else you want to add on the top one? I'll mention one other thing. If you're doing a compact, so a shorter PLS, in the past, I've had a huge amount of success opening a cart on a Friday and then closing a cart at, at midnight on the Monday. And the idea behind that has always been for me, and it seems to have, have played out this way, is on a Friday when the cart opens, it gives people the weekend to think about, you know, what's just happening, what's going to happen when they join and if they should join and they speak to the WAF or, or vice versa. And then on the Monday, it's when everything closes. So then, the tensions all focus back on the Monday. So I've just had lots of success by allowing people to, you know, for these things to play out over a weekend. So just, just over the weekend, Friday through Monday, without, it makes a lot of sense, right? I mean, people, it's easy to get distracted. I mean, how many times have we done that? We're like, Oh, this thing is open, you know, I can buy it anytime over the next two weeks. That's, that's hard. I think that's harder to manage psychologically. I know for me, if, if I get someone out, if I, if I'm pretty certain I'm going to buy something, and it's, I've got two weeks to do it, I'm just going to buy it now, because I just don't want to have to think about it. But in 72 hours, Friday to Monday, that window is nice and short. And, and then I think action, you're, you're, you're basically making a decision, like I'm going to read this email over a weekend, I'm going to buy or not. And it's just not a lot of thought in that, which, you know, makes a lot of sense. And certainly for a first one, for a first PLS, and my, my guess is the temptation for someone doing their first PLS is longer because they want to maximize the possibility of making as much revenue as possible. And counterintuitively, I suspect that the best PLS to do first is shorter. I have a success, get something out there, have a shorter window open and close. It's just, it's just easier to manage, I think. Yeah, I mean, totally. There can be short, short PLSes, even one day PLSes, like the, the times are up, down on my birthday, and they, and they make more money, more revenue in that single day than, than an entire PLS spread out over a week or over, over some, some portion, it just depends on the timing, you know, or the framing coming in. Obviously, if it's a birthday, the people just know that it's, this is only going to happen once a year. So it's not going to come around again for, for an entire year, if at all. So, you know, context matters. And so just, just keep that in mind. Our birthdays are close in time on the calendar too. So you can't do any, it's hard to do back to back PLSes. One thing we didn't include in the, in the module itself, and maybe when you're listening to this now, we've since gone back and added it. But at the time of first recording, something I didn't, we didn't really think to include, which is, we train our audiences what to expect. And there, there are people out there who have a hard close on a PLS, and then you get some nonsense, my opinion, some nonsense email that says something like, oh, you know, we got a bunch of feedback that are such and such link wasn't working on whatever. So we've extended it for three more days. Like, don't do that. Just don't do that. I mean, we could, we could try to be nice about this and say, like, all the reasons why you shouldn't, because, you know, if you train your audience that a hard deadline is a hard deadline, and that does, that doesn't mean if somebody, when somebody reaches out with a legitimate reason, like, I think we had somebody reach out not long ago, his, his wife was having a baby like the day enrollment closed, okay, I got you. All right, like, sure, if people miss it for a legitimate reason, then it's really up to you to make that decision. But if you train your audience to expect that you're, you're kind of, I hate that, but there's no way to put a fine point in this, that you're going to lie to them, right? I mean, sure, in the history of humanity, maybe four or five times something broke at the last day of the launch. But more often than not, that's, it's nonsense. And something, nothing broke. It's just an excuse to add another 72 hours of sales on for some people who you think you could convert with a little extra time. Like, that's, that's really what's going on. If you train your audience to expect that, then you're, I think you're going to weaken the, the PLS in general, if there's this belief that it's, there's always going to be something more, very similar, I think, to the psychology of the, the launch format where people pile on last minute bonuses. Somebody reached out to us during the art of email and the original enrollment and said, he's like, you know, I'm not sure when to, when to buy, you know, when, you know, I think he asked when, when we were going to add the bonuses and if we were going to discount the price for the end. And it was just like, what? But that's, that's what's out in the world. And that's what people expect is like, well, why buy on day one when they'll pile on all the, you know, do a payment plan and all this other stuff on day 10. I mean, if you do that, you do that, I mean, it's, we're not, we're not telling, I want to say we're not telling you not to do that. We're telling you not to do that. There are a million reasons not to do that. It's just easier. It's easier to say this is when it opens, this is when it closes. It's, you know, I'm not going to make up some excuse about why we've added three days. And if a promotion tanks, a promotion tanks, right? That's the reality. Not every promotion is sunshine, sunshine and unicorns. If you get the offer a little bit wrong or the timing a little bit wrong, or you don't explain it, well, just, it happens, learn from it, move on, do your next one. Don't, don't try to tack on another 5% at the expense of how your audience perceives you, because that has long-term consequences. And, you know, I don't, I'm not going to sit down and try to do the math and find the evidence, but my intuition tells me that the long-term consequences of that kind of trickery and nonsense are far more costly than any short-term benefit. That's my hypothesis. Yeah. And you mentioned something that's, that's, that's worth drawing attention to as well, that, you know, you could have a world-class promotion that ticks all the boxes, but you've just done it when the timing is bad. So, broadly speaking, for us, we've, we've learned August is not the best month to do a PLS. So, for us, we're going to rerun it in January, and we hope that it was August. Yeah. So, I mean, timing can be bad sometimes, or there can be a global pandemic that's just, you know, cropped up on day two of your PLS, and now the world's in meltdown. You know, these things happen. So, don't discount the fact that it could have been a really amazing PLS. It's just, you know, stuff intervened, and now it wasn't. But, you know, next time around, you can, you can, you can do it again. Change a few things. Marodi, so, yeah, let's move on to the bottom here. So, what you see on the screen here is really, it's the overview of the initial art of email live workshop. This is when we release the art of email and the live, live version of the workshop simultaneously. And if you look from what, there are a couple of things to draw attention to that I'm going to hand it over to Andre. The first thing to draw attention to is that there are two parallel enrollments happening, and the reason for that is, you'll see segment one, the smaller segment, where people who already owned autoresponder madness, they received the art of email for free, and they were raising their hand only for the live workshop. The larger segment were people who did not own the art of email and were raising their hand for the art of email and the live workshop. So, those two, they were not the same, they were not the same PLS, although they had the same theme, the same narrative arc, which was pride. That was the underlying theme. They had, I believe the first email was almost identical. A lot of the content in different days was very similar, and then other days was completely different, because one audience already owned the art of email. We didn't need to really go into depth about it, although we did want to talk about what was different in the art of email and what was new and through the schedule when we released the content. So, we did talk about the art of email, but the front and center, the PLS itself, was about the live workshop, whereas with the larger segment, we were also talking about the art of email. And you'll see on the left, we had two value newsletters, one on September 10, 2021, the other on the 24th. On the 10th, we mentioned that we would be sending an email the following Tuesday. I think we said two emails. The following Tuesdays, those were hard bridge emails. On the 14th, we just say, hey, it's coming. This is what it's going to look like. And we've sent those to each segment. They go to different one. And then on the 21st, the following Tuesday, we sent another one, that was the art of email manifesto. We sent it to each group, which also had a hard bridge or had a link, an interest list link in it. And on the 24th, we mentioned that we had sent the bridge emails, and we mentioned that the art of email was happening, but there was no interest list link. That was a deliberate decision within that newsletter. We felt like we'd already covered that ground. And then you can see in the actual enrollment itself, we did Monday through Friday, and then the following Monday through Friday, it was 10 emails total, cart opened on the 30th of September on a Thursday and closed the following Friday at midnight Pacific. So this email here, so this is our news that are in the fact that there was no, there was no hard bridge link inside there. As Sean said, that was a decision we made. It is very tempting to have segmentation links, you know, racial hand links all over the place. But one of the things we did, one of the things we said on the 21st, so in these two hard bridge emails, and is this is the last time you can raise your hand for this. So I forget what the wording was, but you know, we, we, it was clear that we weren't going to send another raise your hand email again. So, you know, this is important to us that when we say something, that's what we do. So, which is why there was no automated link inside of here. And there was just in, you know, written in text was, if you have missed it, just hit reply and let us know, and we'll, and we'll get you added. So a lot of people did, if I remember, I mean, there were a significant number of people who either received one of the bridge emails that like they were a customer, but they got the non-customer, like, I mean, that, it does happen, right? And we want to make sure we're not giving anybody the impression that this was just, you know, just flawless, right? And we mentioned it in the 24th because we knew some people hadn't, you know, for whatever reason hadn't really been paying attention. So we did get some replies, but it's nice, it's congruent, right? I think that's something we talk about a lot, that it's, we said we weren't going to do something. So we can't, we can't then do it. Yeah, so it might be obvious or it might not be, but once this thing starts, well, actually, let me backtrack these emails here in the hard bridge, they are, they are there for as long as that email stays in someone's inbox. Somebody can, can, can segment themselves into a one year later, half a long later. And it's going to happen because people aren't always paying attention, the, the moments that the, that the emails hit the inboxes, right? So, and then at this point, when the source starts, these are all manual broadcasts. And that's, that's what a PLS is. So with, with our email service provider, ConvertKit, other service providers operate in a similar manner, as it generates us a, like a archived link that, you know, someone just has to go to that URL and they see the archived version of our email or done automatically. So we have an easy error, we call it easy error, which is a single, a single email sequence inside of ConvertKit. And as soon as somebody segments, they receive that email, which is why it's the easy error. It's, it's the one that goes instantly. And we put that live once we've sent this first email. So we sent, so in this case, Monday the 27th, that email goes out, ConvertKit generates that archived link, and we put that into easy error. And you can see an example of that email inside of the module. And then each subsequent email, we just get the link, we go back to easy error, edit it, and this edit. So anybody segmenting themselves, any, throughout the course of this is always going to be, get that email that has links to all previous emails. So if somebody joins here, they'll have links to everything else, and it just gets them up to speed. And, you know, we, we, we got a significant amount of sales from people coming in late. We share the stats in the module. I don't remember it offhand. But I was surprised when I saw that. I was very surprised how many people had come in late and purchased. Yeah. And the percentage for this segment, yeah, so this segment is our, is, as Sean said, this, this is our Autosmond and Madness customers that were getting out of email for free. So these people already have already demonstrated that, that they know our stuff already, you know, so there's that relationship that already exists. And they're a customer, whereas this segment here, which is more broad, there were many people here that weren't customers of us at all. So so the conversion rate of these guys coming in late was almost 50%. It wasn't far off 50% of people that came in late purchased. So that's, that's pretty significant. So that's why we do the, the easier it's, it's an important nuance that's, that's worth considering. And we did say, you know, we, it's sort of built in suspenders idea too, but we also in the first, I believe it was all, it was at least the first four, maybe the first five, each day that we sent out the first week's email, we would say right in the beginning, if you haven't already done so, please read, you know, day one, two, three, whatever, because we were building a narrative. So we, we don't, we don't want somebody just to jump in midstream. We want, we want to orient them. So having E zero for the late arrivals and having, you know, somebody may have, I mean, if you think about two scenarios here, one obvious scenario, someone raises his or her hand later and they get your easier out. It's like, Hey, here's everything you need to catch up. But there's also the person who sees email for, Hey, enrollments open. They're like, Oh, great. Let me go check it out. But they haven't seen Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday. We want them to see those or at least have the option to go back and see those, even though they've, they, they're already in their inbox. We want to draw attention to, Hey, before you, you know, essentially, if it's the Thursday email, before you think about buying, make sure you kind of go back to the beginning and orient yourself to this. And it's there, there are lots of ways when it's, you know, comes down to this idea, when you care about your audience's experience, then you're looking for ways to engineer the best possible experience. Now, in two, two ways, the experience can go wrong. It's if somebody hasn't been paying attention, and they just jump in and see an email, and they have the rest are in their inbox, and we can link to the other emails as a nice touch. And somebody comes in later, and they don't have those emails in their inbox to say, Hey, here's what you've missed. This is how you can orient yourself instead of saying, Hey, glad you're here, let's see if we can sell you something. Right. Like that's, you know, that's a different vibe. One thing I'll draw attention to as well, that we don't have to go too deep on this, but because this is a PLS, this is I think really important, which is we did not write 10 emails in advance. We would write the emails, usually the day before sometimes editing the morning of occasionally, I think once or twice, writing the morning of the day that they were sent, because a PLS is dynamic, we're engaged in a conversation with our audience. For example, when we sent the first email about pride, we clearly struck a chord because we got so many replies back. And then you'll see many of those replies quoted in subsequent emails, or as after a card opened, and people began to engage with module one content, we were seeing comments on that content, which we were included. We also had one of our emails, I believe was Monday the fourth was an email in response to it was very clear, we had not explained something well, or thoroughly about the workshop and that Monday email, like was for an opportunity to go back and look through a week's worth of questions and see like, okay, we haven't, we clearly haven't answered this well yet. So let's just answer this question as best that we can, and this email and make sure that our audience understands it. So the entire PLS, very dynamic. And, you know, sure, you could write a couple of days ahead if you need to do that for scheduling, but I would certainly discourage anyone from trying to write a PLS start to finish in advance, because there's a there's a beautiful energy for the audience, where you're sending out signals and emails, and you're getting signals back, and you're seeing what's working, and you're getting a sense for what isn't, and to be able to continuously reorient to that and iterate, it's very powerful aspect of a PLS. Yeah, exactly. I think the only use case where you can pre-write your entire PLS is if it's a one day promotion on Andre's birthday. Right. Well, and it's funny too, because last year we did, for your birthday, we did a weekend promotion, and I wrote it because it was your birthday, and we sort of did the whole thing, but even that, like Sunday, Sunday and Monday's email, I didn't write until Sunday and Monday, because, you know, there was so much feedback coming in, and there was just there was so much happening, that even that when I didn't feel like I could just walk away and have those two things ready, I mean, it was it's fascinating. Do you want to talk about the, we talk about it in the module, do you want to talk at all about the free course and sort of how we think about that, and why we do that, I don't know if that's worth including here, and we did that on on the Thursday the 7th, it's funny, I guess there's another point to make here too, which is no plan survives contact with the reality. So our initial plan was we were sending emails Monday through Friday, week one, and then Monday, Wednesday, Friday, week two, and then that plan became Monday through Friday, week one, Monday, Wednesday, Thursday for the free course, and then Friday, and then by the following Monday, we realized it was just going to be Monday through Friday, then Monday through Friday, because there was a there was something we wanted to share about the different reasons people buy that we thought was just valuable for people reading the series that is like, okay, I feel bad if we didn't include this, so there's a Tuesday email now. So do you want to talk about any of that like the free course and sort of how that how that fits into is it so counterintuitive, I know that people are reading this like why would you depress conversion? Like it makes no it seems to make no sense. But it only the reason it doesn't make sense is when the timeframe is short, right, if you're like, oh, in this, in this promotion, if I give away something for free at the end, I'm likely to reduce conversion. Like that's what most people think. But what they mean is I'm likely to reduce conversion in this promotion, not thinking about the effects that might have over the long term, which we believe is advantageous. Like we think that if we do this consistently, that the long term effect is that people will buy when it's right for them. And they'll not when we're trying to almost force the issue with persuasion or coercion or whatever you want to call it. But that there's something about that seems like a net positive over time. Anything to add to that? I don't know if that's even worth talking about in this recording. I think it's worth, I think everybody should at least try once and just see how it works for you. I mean, we've been doing it now for the best part of 18 months. We've give the little free thing that's very valuable. And we give it away on the last day. And in this last example, we did it, we gave it away on the Thursday, so the day before. So I think it's definitely worth testing for yourselves. Just don't necessarily measure it on on sales because it's it can be misleading and it's difficult to do. Try and do it based on people responding and letting you know if that was a cool thing for you to do. But the large majority of people are not going to bar. So when we get 20% of our segments, so these segments here that you create in most cases, you'll have a single segment. Well, when we get 20% of that entire segment purchasing, well, that still means 80% did not purchase. So our little thing that we give them is for everybody, but it's especially for those, for that 80% that that for whatever reason, this just wasn't a good fit or wasn't a good fit in this moment. But they still wanted to be part of the of the experience. And maybe along the way, they figured out actually, timing isn't isn't best. I wanted to do this now, but maybe not now. And they kind of convinced themselves of that. And they just, you know, they're going to do it next time or the time after that. But, you know, there's a there's a significant amount of people that that won't purchase. And I think they appreciate getting this this little thing at the end. So that's something that that we do. And it's, I think we'll continue to do it. Yeah, and I think it's worth pointing out too, this isn't, this isn't trickery, right? It's so much of it of internet marketing ends up being and what I consider just trickery. It's like, okay, so do this thing and give away a free thing because it'll increase your sale. Like, that's not why we're doing this. We have no idea. We have no data that suggests that over an 18 months period, we've generated a single additional sale because of this thing that we're doing. We're doing it because we think it's the right thing to do, right? People have paid attention for five days, seven days, 10 days. Most of them who've paid attention have not purchased. So, and the one thing that they can't get more of is their attention. You can always get more money. They can always, they can't, attention's finite and they've been giving us their attention. So, to give something back, it just, it seems like the right thing to do. And if it doesn't generate a single additional sale, who cares? That's not why we're doing it. And the reason we did this initially is when we were doing the traffic engine promotion, we were writing individually and there was an energy around it. Like, there was just like, we could, it was palpable. And when we got to the second, I think it was Sunday and then enrollment closed on Tuesday, we got to Sunday. I had this, like, all of a sudden had a very significant concern that we had had this experience that was just so exciting for, certainly for us. And based on the feedback, people were really engaged with it. And for knowing that most of the people after Tuesday, it was just over, right? Like, that whole experience was coming to an end if they didn't purchase. And for people who purchased, over the next eight weeks, they were going to, the traffic engine was going to be revealed, you know, module by module over the next eight weeks and everybody else who had participated, that wasn't going to happen. So the original mini marketing MBA was designed to be an eight week course that the people who didn't buy could have something over the next eight weeks. That like, it was not, there was never this thought like, oh, and if we weren't business partners at that point, this was, this wasn't some thing like, oh, over the next two years, this is really going to pay off. It's just sometimes you just do things often, you just do things as it's the right thing to do. Like, it just felt like the right thing to do. So I mentioned this was I don't want anybody to read into this as like, oh, this is, you know, part of a basket of, you know, a toolkit that we have to increase long term sales. If that happens, it's an emergent property of doing the right thing. And that's, that's the beginning and the end of it. So don't feel like you need to add this to your toolbox of things, because we do it, it may not fit for you. And if it doesn't, that's fine, you're not missing out on something. Just, just be aware that this is an option. And it's something that you can adapt to your own specific situation. All right, this is the long one today. Do you want to, you want to take us, take us home and wrap things up? Anything else we should draw attention to on the PLS? I'll take it home, mentioning one final thing, which is at the end of this module, we, we are unpack, we're unpack why we do this and, and a few options around it, which is the world, world building component of, of doing a PLS. And so one of the things we do is some of the emails, sometimes all the emails, sometimes just some of the emails, we will put onto our website. So this is external to our email service provider generating us these, these shareable links of the emails, which we put into the EZero. We also take the time and energy to actually repurpose them as, as proper content pieces on our website of the emails. And sometimes we repurpose those emails and we'll, we'll modify them slightly because obviously these are part of a promotion. So, you know, we don't want the emails on our website necessarily to be part of a promotion. So we'll repurpose them based on the, the narrative or the series or some of the content pieces inside of here, where it makes sense. And we'll, we'll put that on the website as our world building, because people will, people will farm this content over time and it's, it's, it's a flywheel. They'll, they'll, they'll be pulled in by the content and they'll opt in into our OBS somewhere and they'll receive our newsletters and they'll be part of some future PLS. And that's all because of the world building effects. And we do that with our, with our, with our value news that are as well. That's, that's not part of this, this, this module, but you know, the world building effects and what we do is constant with everything we do. And also, like when we did the, did the traffic engine, I think we, we archived on our website, all of those emails, every single one of them, including the final email, which is the free gift. So people have found that eight week masterclass free gift, um, by browsing on the website and they've just stumbled upon, the, the traffic engine and they've gone in there and they've read the email. And you know, it's, it's going to have an effect on them. And they get to see this like, wow, this is what, what these guys do. And they can enjoy the benefits of it, even though they had nothing to do with this promotion. Maybe they'll be part of some future promotion, or they'll just go and make a purchase because, you know, the traffic engine is now available. And if you look in bar, whenever they want. So it's these second and third order consequences. And you know, these things happen. It's, it's all emergent. We contract every single thing. You know, we just do things that feel right to me, put it out there. And this whole thing just, just works like that. So I think that's, that's, that's, that's how in one piece of, that sounds good. That's a perfect way to end this module. And like we keep saying, now that you've heard the content, it's time to get back to work.
